speaker: Sean Farrington
text: Hello, and welcome to the Big Boss interview. I'm Sean Farrington,

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: and this time round, I've been chatting to the boss of one of our biggest house builders in the UK.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: This is Barrett Redrow, the company, and he, the chief executive, is David Thomas.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: And we had a lot to get through.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: Unsurprisingly, anybody who has ever thought about the housing market wanting to get on the housing ladder, owning a home, looking to move, looking to upgrade, the consequences on the economy if you're working in it on a building site.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: We spoke about all of that.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: He had some really interesting stuff to say about the future of the building site in a world of artificial intelligence and a world of

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: difficulty for many industries to get the skills they need.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: We've heard about the consequences that that could have on how homes are built and the amount of people working on those building sites.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: Never mind in heat, as some of you listening to this may have been experiencing in recent days extreme temperatures, are new build homes fit for purpose.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: What might the future look like for them if we're going to get more temperatures like we've had in recent times?

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: Just before we recorded this particular episode, and as well,

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: we spoke about the consequences of higher interest rates.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: What the world looks like for first-time buyers.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: How that compares to just after the financial crisis when he actually first joined Barrett 17 years on.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: Is the world any different for first-time buyers out there?

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: What's got better, what's got worse?

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: We spoke about all of that, plenty more.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: So here is my conversation with David Thomas, the chief executive of Barrett Redrow.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: Welcome to Big Boss interview.

speaker: David Thomas
text: Thank you, Sean. I'm really delighted to be with you this morning.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: It's uh always an interesting time to talk about housing.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: You could you pluck any point and there'd be a topical moment of the week.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: In the in the week that we're talking right now, people will have been having a few sweaty nights and and hot days and wondering what to do with their homes.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: We've had air conditioning installers tell us that they've seen record levels of interest.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: How much of that becomes a hot topic for you when you're thinking about the homes you need to be building?

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: Are the homes we're building fit for the climate changes that we might have in the the months, the decades ahead?

speaker: David Thomas
text: Well, I think first of all to put it in context as everyone knows, we've clearly seen dramatic changes in climate.

speaker: David Thomas
text: If you look over the last 10 years, 20 years, 30 years,

speaker: David Thomas
text: so we've got to continue to adapt the homes that we build.

speaker: David Thomas
text: in the UK as a country, we're not building anything like enough homes.

speaker: David Thomas
text: But the government have set out a very clear road map in terms of the way that we're going to address

speaker: David Thomas
text: uh future home standard.

speaker: David Thomas
text: The future home standard has been set, which will mean that we'll move away from gas central heating to heat pumps.

speaker: David Thomas
text: Uh air source heat pumps will generally be the preferred methodology.

speaker: David Thomas
text: And we're always looking at ways in which homes can be better insulated so that homes are both warm enough,

speaker: David Thomas
text: but don't overheat during these warm spells.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: Are you finding that the homes that have been built over the last decade or so aren't actually quite suitable for the extreme high temperatures that we're seeing sort of a little bit more frequently over the course of our summers and sometimes springs?

speaker: David Thomas
text: Well, this is an area that we've done a lot of work on.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So in conjunction with the University of Salford, we built a a home, the energy house two in a climate chamber at the University of Salford. So that climate chamber allows the temperature to be taken down to minus 20 and up to plus 40.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So looking at the way that the house responds to those conditions is very, very important.

speaker: David Thomas
text: And unquestionably, homes that were built 20 years ago, 30 years ago, or even 10 years ago,

speaker: David Thomas
text: um they they do find it tough to perform if temperatures are going up to 30, 40 degrees.

speaker: David Thomas
text: And we we've clearly seen those temperatures recently and we've seen 40 degree temperatures be breached in London over the last few years.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So we've got to continue to develop and continue to innovate, which is very much in line with the government roadmap.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: Are you a bit worried though that that houses that are being built right now, you almost know,

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: are not fit for for 30 plus temperatures on a regular basis.

speaker: David Thomas
text: No, I don't I wouldn't say that that's a concern.

speaker: David Thomas
text: I think we've got to recognize that we we need to continue to develop and continue to innovate.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So if you look at the way that homes have been developed over the last 10 or 20 years,

speaker: David Thomas
text: there's been a huge amount of improvement in terms of the thermal efficiency of those homes, and that improvement is going to continue over the next three years, five years, 10 years.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So it's a it's a gradual process, but if I look at the homes that we're building now,

speaker: David Thomas
text: compared to the homes that we built two decades ago or three decades ago, there's a there's a massive difference in the performance of those homes.

speaker: David Thomas
text: Both in cold temperatures and also in warm temperatures.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: Are you seeing more inquiries or more complaints from people who've bought homes when the high temperatures come in?

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: I just wonder if this is becoming an an increasing issue for people who are actually buying these homes.

speaker: David Thomas
text: I I wouldn't say that we're seeing more issues being raised by customers

speaker: David Thomas
text: who have bought homes over the last three years, five years.

speaker: David Thomas
text: I I don't think that is really a feature.

speaker: David Thomas
text: I think to a large extent almost the reverse in that as we've seen energy costs

speaker: David Thomas
text: and the costs of running homes rising so dramatically over the last four or five years in particular,

speaker: David Thomas
text: what we're seeing is a lot more interest from the customer in the opportunity for lower running costs.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So whether that be the the costs of heating the home, or the water consumption of the home,

speaker: David Thomas
text: I think all consumers are interested in savings that can be achieved through new build homes.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So that's a very, very important part of our differentiation in the differentiation for the new build industry relative to uh the existing homes in the marketplace.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: What might be different in the future, you know, as you go through your projects with the University of Salford that you talk about?

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: That that that may be something that is going to be increasingly in new build homes in the future,

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: to be able to handle, you know, both extremes of temperatures.

speaker: David Thomas
text: Yeah, so so I think it's a it's a revolution that we're seeing in terms of the future home standard.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So we've we've done work as I said with the University of Salford that the industry cooperates with government through the future homes hub.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So the future homes hub is a collaboration between the industry and government. I chaired the future homes hub for three years up until April this year.

speaker: David Thomas
text: When you look at the innovation that is going to come through into production, it is quite dramatic. So,

speaker: David Thomas
text: I touched on the fact that we're going to move away from gas central heating, um and moving away from gas central heating.

speaker: David Thomas
text: The most likely innovation there will be air source heat pumps.

speaker: David Thomas
text: Air source heat pumps are then bringing in the opportunity for underfloor heating, particularly on the ground floor to become a standard.

speaker: David Thomas
text: We then see that in terms of further improving thermal efficiency that something like triple glazing is going to become much more the norm.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So addressing both the um cooling properties of the home and and also the the the heating qualities of the home.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: But those things you mentioned, is it fair to say they're more about the heating qualities of the home rather than the cooling properties?

speaker: David Thomas
text: I I I think that um both. I mean, I I I think that triple glazing can help in that regard.

speaker: David Thomas
text: I think with heat pumps there is the opportunity to introduce with heat pumps um air conditioning.

speaker: David Thomas
text: Now that's unlikely to be brought in as a standard,

speaker: David Thomas
text: but that can be brought in as a modification.

speaker: David Thomas
text: Uh I saw the conservatives announce something yesterday or today about air conditioning being a standard in the future.

speaker: David Thomas
text: But I think Do you think it should be a standard in the future?

speaker: David Thomas
text: I not at this point, no.

speaker: David Thomas
text: Because I I I think that we're not designing as a rule. I think you're not designing for extremes.

speaker: David Thomas
text: And therefore the utilization of that relative to the cost may not be a good proposition for a lot of customers.

speaker: David Thomas
text: Whereas I think when you look at the heat pump relative to gas for various reasons,

speaker: David Thomas
text: for sustainability reasons, for efficiency reasons, I think that is a very good response for the customer.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: Does that put up the cost of a of a house if you have a heat pump if that becomes a standard? Is that mean that a new build is going to be higher than it would have been without one?

speaker: David Thomas
text: When you look at uh the heat pump relative to a gas boiler,

speaker: David Thomas
text: at this point, a heat pump would be more expensive than the installation of a gas boiler.

speaker: David Thomas
text: Now it depend on the size of the home and the heat pump required.

speaker: David Thomas
text: But you you could be easily talking of four or 5,000 pound differential.

speaker: David Thomas
text: But the reality is that the cost of heat pumps will fall as we move into more of a mass production environment,

speaker: David Thomas
text: then those costs will fall.

speaker: David Thomas
text: And I think the best example we've seen of that in recent years has been solar panels.

speaker: David Thomas
text: You know, when I first came into the business 17 years ago, we were looking at the viability around solar panels,

speaker: David Thomas
text: and the unit cost of solar panels has fallen dramatically as more and more solar panels are installed in the market.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: Do you think people are getting a bit wary of of having to pay a bit more for some technology now,

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: for it one day to be cheaper for others, and this is maybe a cynical way of putting it.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: By the time you get to those heat pumps being cheaper, there'll be another bit of technology that we're being told, actually,

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: you need to pay more for this now, and it'll be cheaper one day.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: And it's just been a bit of a relentless ride.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: You've been in charge at Barrett for quite a time now.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: You will have seen this.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: Is it has it been a relentless ride of ever increasing costs?

speaker: David Thomas
text: Well, I I think maybe to to separate two points there.

speaker: David Thomas
text: I mean, first of all in terms of the customer approach, we would say that the customer,

speaker: David Thomas
text: if they can see a saving, they'll tend to look at an investment for that saving

speaker: David Thomas
text: over a four or five year period.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So if you're going to save a thousand pounds, then you're prepared to invest a capital amount of four or 5,000 pounds.

speaker: David Thomas
text: What's happened in terms of the cost of living and the let's say energy crisis over the last few years,

speaker: David Thomas
text: is that the costs of running and heating homes has risen dramatically.

speaker: David Thomas
text: And therefore I think customers are more willing to pay in terms of the the costs of, for example, a heat pump.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So, ultimately I think it ends up being a shared cost.

speaker: David Thomas
text: That some of the costs comes to the house builder, some of the costs comes to the customer,

speaker: David Thomas
text: but we're very, very confident that we're delivering a better experience overall.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: Given the technologies that are out there, the skills that are out there now for energy efficiencies,

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: how are we still at a point even with older houses,

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: where so many don't have the insulation needed to drastically reduce people's energy costs,

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: when we're worried about oil prices all around the world?

speaker: David Thomas
text: Well, I mean, I think in terms of new build, there are dramatic differentials in relation to the let's say the average secondhand home.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So that has been you know, an advantage as such for new build over the last few years, because those differentials have increased.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So just as an illustration, if you went back five or six years ago,

speaker: David Thomas
text: we would have been talking about the differential cost being maybe five or 600 pounds per annum.

speaker: David Thomas
text: Whereas there's been points over the last few years, the host of the war in Ukraine, where the differential has been 2, 2 and a half thousand pounds per annum.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: On energy bills in a home compared to a second second hand compared to a second hand equivalent.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So so these are these are very dramatic increases.

speaker: David Thomas
text: I mean, just to go back on the other point that you made in terms of let's say increasing costs.

speaker: David Thomas
text: And uh I know that when you get onto this, it it it makes me sound old.

speaker: David Thomas
text: But the reality is look, the cost of everything is ever increasing.

speaker: David Thomas
text: But if you look over the last four or five years, we have seen dramatic increases in terms of the cost of building homes.

speaker: David Thomas
text: Coming from two main areas, one from sheer inflationary costs, and again coming from

speaker: David Thomas
text: the budgetary events in 2022 coming from the war in Ukraine, we've seen dramatic inflation on build costs.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So, um gas prices, steel prices and so on have fueled those increases.

speaker: David Thomas
text: And then we've seen very significant increases from a regulatory point of view.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So,

speaker: David Thomas
text: environmental regulation, which we see as being very important and our customer sees as being very important, but the reality is it comes at a cost.

speaker: David Thomas
text: And our trade body, the Home Builders Federation published some data recently saying that costs had risen by around 75,000 pounds for a typical home over the last five years.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So those are those are dramatic increases in build costs and and it makes it a, you know, a tough environment to operate in.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: But yet, isn't the you know, the policy of this government been build baby build.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: Was that the quote at one point we were told over and over?

speaker: David Thomas
text: Yeah, I mean, look, we've welcomed, you can understand that all we do, all I do is new build housing.

speaker: David Thomas
text: All of our teams.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So to have a government that is focused on increasing the delivery of housing numbers,

speaker: David Thomas
text: and let's say improving the environmental efficiency of the homes has got to be a good thing.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: Are we increasing the delivery of homes?

speaker: David Thomas
text: Well, I think that the government has set out very clear plans in terms of improving the supply side.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So focusing on uh particularly around planning.

speaker: David Thomas
text: I I I don't think this is an issue just for new build homes.

speaker: David Thomas
text: I think for all aspects of our economy, you know, whether you're trying to build a new hotel, a new store, you're trying to build a reservoir,

speaker: David Thomas
text: the reality is that planning has acted as a very significant constraint.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So freeing up the supply side in terms of planning is very, very important.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: Is that working?

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: Are you seeing that?

speaker: David Thomas
text: Well, I mean, we need to bear in mind that the legislation only came into effect in December 25.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So the reality the reality is it is relatively early days.

speaker: David Thomas
text: But I would be very confident that we will see planning numbers rise.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So the reality is as we look through 26 and 27, we will see planning numbers take up over that period of time.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So I think it's right for the government to address the supply side.

speaker: David Thomas
text: I think it is right for the government to have ambitious targets.

speaker: David Thomas
text: I mean, when you look at our housing delivery over the last 50 years,

speaker: David Thomas
text: I mean, really going back to the early 80s, we have undersupplied the market dramatically over that period of time.

speaker: David Thomas
text: And hence if you look at sort of headline stats on housing,

speaker: David Thomas
text: you know, house prices have gone up about three times faster than anything else.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So we've seen dramatic increases in house prices in real terms, um, you know, even when you're adjusting for underlying inflation in the market.

speaker: David Thomas
text: And that is largely due to a lack of supply.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So a long-term focus on the supply side of the equation, I think is very, very important.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: What about the other side of that? The demand side of it?

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: What's going on with people wanting to buy houses at the minute? Where are you seeing stronger demand and weaker demand?

speaker: David Thomas
text: I think the, um, you know, we kind of update the market on a fairly regular basis.

speaker: David Thomas
text: And I think it's become a very repetitive, um, commentary that

speaker: David Thomas
text: London in particular, London and the Southeast sees huge challenges in terms of affordability.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So I'm not saying that elsewhere in the country there aren't challenges,

speaker: David Thomas
text: but the challenges are most extreme in London and the Southeast.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So for first-time buyers trying to get onto the property ladder, that is a very, very tough equation to solve.

speaker: David Thomas
text: We will try to do what we can in terms of offers and incentives for the customer.

speaker: David Thomas
text: But the reality is when you look at average house prices compared to average salaries, then there are very, very significant challenges.

speaker: David Thomas
text: And that is simply a byproduct of a lack of supply over a long period of time.

speaker: David Thomas
text: Making houses in Europe relative to salaries, uh some of the very highest levels in Europe.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: But yet when we look at how prices have moved around the country, many might feel that actually those in London haven't seen the house price growth and in some areas seen a fall in prices in recent times.

speaker: David Thomas
text: That that is very true over recent times.

speaker: David Thomas
text: But I think if you're looking at, you know, house price movements say since 2006 or 2007, London will have seen very dramatic movements in house prices

speaker: David Thomas
text: And other parts of the country have only just got back or in some cases haven't got back to 2007 levels.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So I think there has been a very, very mixed geographic position if you look over the last five years, 10 years, 20 years.

speaker: David Thomas
text: But as a general rule, the affordability challenges are much greater in London than they are elsewhere in the country.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: If you're a a younger person in the UK and you're looking to get on the housing market in your life,

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: have the prospects in the last decade improved for you or not getting on the housing ladder?

speaker: David Thomas
text: I think it has definitely become more challenging recently, so if you look over the last three or four years, then affordability has become a much bigger challenge.

speaker: David Thomas
text: Clearly, there's been movements in interest rates, uh far uh

speaker: David Thomas
text: higher interest rates than we'd seen previously, um there's been a lot of underlying inflation and salaries haven't necessarily kept pace.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So all all of those things are feeding into more challenges around affordability.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So therefore you can see that uh for first-time buyers getting onto the housing ladder, the the the stats are falling.

speaker: David Thomas
text: And the reality is less people are getting onto the housing ladder.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: When when you say salaries haven't necessarily been keeping a pace.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: I mean, when we have looked in the last few years at how much wages have gone up by, they've gone up by considerable amounts on average.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: Now we're starting to see that wage growth actually gets smaller on average.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: You're feeling like it they weren't necessarily keeping up in the first place.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: What are the consequences of this of what's happening in the in the labor market right now for the housing market?

speaker: David Thomas
text: I think the consequences are very challenging for the housing market.

speaker: David Thomas
text: I mean, we would look at the affordability equation on a very, very regular basis and you can see that affordability has deteriorated.

speaker: David Thomas
text: I mean, there's been slight improvements as interest rates have fallen, but then we've obviously seen recent rises in interest rates.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So affordability has deteriorated.

speaker: David Thomas
text: I think the other area that for younger people, uh which proves challenging is where people are carrying student debt.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So that is another factor to feed into the affordability equation in terms of deductions that are already coming from salaries.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: How is that student debt impacting when people buy, how they buy, if they buy?

speaker: David Thomas
text: I think as a generalization, it's just making it more challenging, you know, once people are earning a certain level of salary.

speaker: David Thomas
text: They have a requirement to repay student debt and therefore their available earnings as assessed by the bank for mortgage purposes will be lower.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So inevitably, the pool of people that can afford is is reduced by that underlying debt position.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So of this means that affordability is more challenged.

speaker: David Thomas
text: And you know, we've said that we do believe that the government should put in place some demand package,

speaker: David Thomas
text: particularly focused for first-time buyers.

speaker: David Thomas
text: Now, the house builders have said consistently that they would be happy to contribute towards that.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So it's not we're not looking for something for nothing.

speaker: David Thomas
text: But the reality is that there are very, very big implications for the country,

speaker: David Thomas
text: if people are not getting onto the housing ladder and are going to rent on a permanent basis.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So home ownership in terms of the building of the homes, in terms of people owning their own homes, has big benefits for the country.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: But one of the concerns about having policies that that may be like that, people might think that sounds a little bit like help to buy that was around for a while,

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: is that actually, when all is said and done and the maths are done all these years later,

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: the Institute for Fiscal Studies did some analysis recently, it was wealthier households,

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: wealthier individuals who are actually the ones taking advantage of these schemes,

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: and not those who are finding it hardest to get on the ladder.

speaker: David Thomas
text: Yeah, so I think we've, you know, we've said that the we see that we do see the schemes as being important.

speaker: David Thomas
text: You know, the reality is that absent these schemes as a country, we're we're creating big generational inequalities.

speaker: David Thomas
text: I think, you know, if I look at myself, I bought for the first time in in the 1980s.

speaker: David Thomas
text: And we had the scheme that operated for a period of time in the 1980s and provided very significant support to buyers.

speaker: David Thomas
text: If you look through the 90s and the 2000s and so on,

speaker: David Thomas
text: we've had government schemes running on a very, very regular basis.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So this is not

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: Not new,

speaker: David Thomas
text: help to buy

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: but people might be thinking,

speaker: David Thomas
text: wasn't something new.

speaker: David Thomas
text: We had uh first buy prior to that.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: Not new,

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: but people might be thinking,

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: well, you've had all these schemes and yet here we still are all these decades on with the housing market that isn't working for the country.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: Doesn't there need to be something a bit more radical than just a a continuation of these schemes that are healthing?

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: helping those with some wealth or assets in the first place to get to get on the ladder or higher up the ladder?

speaker: David Thomas
text: I I think that, you know, comes back to pulling both levers, you know, the government are presently very aggressively pursuing the supply side.

speaker: David Thomas
text: And and we've said right right from the middle of 2014,

speaker: David Thomas
text: when the government came into power that we think addressing the planning side is fundamentally important.

speaker: David Thomas
text: If you only pull the lever on the demand side,

speaker: David Thomas
text: then clearly you're not solving the problem.

speaker: David Thomas
text: But I think you've got to pull two levers for a period of time.

speaker: David Thomas
text: Because if you don't, then you'll leave a whole generation behind, and that I I think is an absolutely key point.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: Is there a risk of a whole generation being left behind?

speaker: David Thomas
text: Of course.

speaker: David Thomas
text: Of course there is.

speaker: David Thomas
text: You know, if you look at the way that housing purchases, housing starts, home ownership for first-time buyers.

speaker: David Thomas
text: The way that the age of the, you know, the average first-time buyer is increasing and increasing,

speaker: David Thomas
text: these are all factors that that lead towards generational inequalities.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: Put it in a bit of context, given, you know, you've been at Barrett and Barrett Redroad for 17 years, large chunk of that is chief executive. Is this the worst time ever to be a first-time buyer?

speaker: David Thomas
text: It's it's certainly one of the most challenging times.

speaker: David Thomas
text: I I I I'm not sure I would try to decide on the worst time ever.

speaker: David Thomas
text: But but certainly it's going to be close to where we were in terms of the post the great financial crisis.

speaker: David Thomas
text: Now that was probably more to do with lending.

speaker: David Thomas
text: um coming out of 2008, 2009.

speaker: David Thomas
text: But but I think it's very, very comparable for first-time buyers in particularly when you look at areas like London and the Southeast.

speaker: David Thomas
text: There was immediately government support put in place in 2009 and thereafter. So,

speaker: David Thomas
text: we're now facing challenges around affordability with with no government support scheme in place.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So, yeah, challenging, very, very difficult for first-time buyers.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: And you know, we many started this year thinking interest rates would fall and the war in Iran began at the end of February.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: And we've heard how expectations of interest rates and where they will go have changed throughout the year.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: depending on who you ask, they might stay about where they are, some think they might go up.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: How has that changed the prospects for a young buyer, a first-time buyer this year?

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: How what's the war in Iran done to those prospects?

speaker: David Thomas
text: I think it's been a really dramatic change.

speaker: David Thomas
text: You know, unfortunately, I think if you went back over the last five or six years,

speaker: David Thomas
text: starting the year and being given a certain message,

speaker: David Thomas
text: and then finding during the year that message has changed,

speaker: David Thomas
text: has become more the norm.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So if you go back to February as you said,

speaker: David Thomas
text: you know, we were doing our half year results in February.

speaker: David Thomas
text: We were expecting an interest rate reduction in March.

speaker: David Thomas
text: The percentage probability of a rate reduction in March, as I remember was kind of north of 80%.

speaker: David Thomas
text: With a further reduction to come in September.

speaker: David Thomas
text: And in practice, we've seen a significant rise in if you look at the two-year fix.

speaker: David Thomas
text: We've seen a significant rise as the conflict in the Middle East has taken off.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So if you're a first-time buyer,

speaker: David Thomas
text: then clearly that's a very dramatically different backdrop now surrounded with a lot of uncertainty.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: Do you think the Bank of England has

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: got its message across clearly? Do you know what its plan is?

speaker: David Thomas
text: I think I mean, from what I can see, the messaging from the Bank of England has been clear.

speaker: David Thomas
text: But clearly, the Bank of England's position has a has altered dramatically over the last quarter.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So, you know, they can only respond to what's in front of them.

speaker: David Thomas
text: And I think they fully understand the impact of interest rate increases, but equally, they understand the impact of inflation.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So they can only respond to that as it unfolds.

speaker: David Thomas
text: But the reality is for the first-time buyer, it's a very, very different message and this variability in the messaging

speaker: David Thomas
text: is a big, big challenge for a first-time buyer.

speaker: David Thomas
text: And it goes back to

speaker: David Thomas
text: you know, demand side support would definitely help with that because it would it would help to smooth the affordability challenges that are faced.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: Whether it's young people working in your business, young people you may be selling homes to,

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: relatives you may be have.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: How how how do you reflect on the world

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: that is in front of them and their prospects at the moment?

speaker: David Thomas
text: Well, I think the reality is, I mean, I would probably always as an individual,

speaker: David Thomas
text: be reasonably cautious.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So I think if you're giving advice to people that I think being reasonably cautious.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So when you look at people, for example, thinking about moving jobs, thinking about which job they should take on,

speaker: David Thomas
text: then, you know, there's a degree of caution and we see that in our business.

speaker: David Thomas
text: That I would say a lot of people are probably looking at things and thinking now is a good time just to sit tight.

speaker: David Thomas
text: Not not necessarily to move on at this point because prospects can be uncertain in new companies.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So I think that can slow down the labor market, for example.

speaker: David Thomas
text: People making decisions about buying a home.

speaker: David Thomas
text: If they're seeing house prices stable or house prices falling, that that's not a call to action.

speaker: David Thomas
text: That's a call just to sit and wait and see what happens.

speaker: David Thomas
text: And so that I think is the position that we're finding ourselves moving into.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: A lot of people might think,

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: lower house prices is what I've always wanted.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: I know I get told by those building homes and selling them,

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: that that that won't help.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: And I hear from everybody who already owns a home, I don't want house prices falling.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: But actually for a a large chunk of the country, don't they just want houses to be a lot, lot cheaper?

speaker: David Thomas
text: I think when you're when you're you know, you you really captured it there.

speaker: David Thomas
text: You know, when you're buying a house and you're coming onto the property ladder for the first time, then having house prices that are affordable is very, very important.

speaker: David Thomas
text: Now, I understand that people are invested in housing in that they own a family house, and they've owned a family house for a long period of time.

speaker: David Thomas
text: And that comes back to what I said about the the generational position.

speaker: David Thomas
text: You know, that housing for a lot of generations over the last 50 years has been an appreciating asset of some substance.

speaker: David Thomas
text: And you you'll see that contradiction in terms of people feeling, for example, that if more homes are built, that that's not good for them and for their house price.

speaker: David Thomas
text: But on the other hand, they want their children or their relatives to be able to own properties.

speaker: David Thomas
text: And therefore, properties need to be affordable.

speaker: David Thomas
text: But the only way that can be addressed is through a long-term solution, as I said, in terms of supply and demand.

speaker: David Thomas
text: I mean, we've we've taken 50 years to get ourselves into this position.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So the reality is it's going to take a long time for us to get ourselves out of this position, um by by uh providing more more homes that the country needs.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: Many might feel that actually the house builders, you know, aren't particularly incentivized to be building the homes that people need and where they need them.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: And actually, you know, the ability of you to hold on to land for a lengthy period of time and not building it as soon as you get it is is part of the problem here.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: The the industry, for all of you described the challenges the industry has, there's actually some practices in the industry that are really not helping.

speaker: David Thomas
text: Yeah, and I I look, I I I understand the criticism and the reality is for house builder and, you know, so this for for Barrett Redro, you know, it's in our DNA.

speaker: David Thomas
text: I mean, what we want to do is we want to build and sell homes.

speaker: David Thomas
text: We've demonstrated over a long period of time, we've been in business since 1958.

speaker: David Thomas
text: We listed on the stock market in 1968, and we've demonstrated over a long period of time that we are a business

speaker: David Thomas
text: that is trying to deliver high volumes of homes, great quality homes, great customer service,

speaker: David Thomas
text: but high volumes of homes into the marketplace, and through all of our brands, through Barrett, through David Wilson and through Redro.

speaker: David Thomas
text: That's what we're set to do.

speaker: David Thomas
text: You know, we we set out a very clear growth plan,

speaker: David Thomas
text: back in February 2015 after our combination with Redro.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So you can look at our history and say, look, we are on the front foot in terms of housing delivery.

speaker: David Thomas
text: We're not trying to hold land for land to appreciate, we can run a very effective model,

speaker: David Thomas
text: building and selling homes rapidly.

speaker: David Thomas
text: And that's what we're absolutely focused on.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: So what's the biggest blocker then? Given there's energy from the government,

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: people want to buy want to buy homes and get to that point.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: You've got the land.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: What's stopping you building?

speaker: David Thomas
text: The the biggest blocker is about affordability.

speaker: David Thomas
text: You know, who who can afford to buy, who can secure mortgages that allow them to get onto the housing ladder?

speaker: David Thomas
text: That's the big blocker.

speaker: David Thomas
text: And it,

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: But within that,

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: that that's a that's a scheme.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: You've already you've mentioned schemes that are that may well be needed.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: So that's some support from somewhere.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: You've mentioned how expensive it is to build homes.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: Um in in terms of affordability, interest rates.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: They're higher than they were.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: is is it is it just an industry that needs to be on life support

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: the whole time and always having schemes that are coming?

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: And just it it doesn't really feel like there's a an actual solution being laid out here

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: to build more homes.

speaker: David Thomas
text: Well, as I say, I I I definitely think the solution is about both levers.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So the supply side, very, very important.

speaker: David Thomas
text: The supply side is being addressed.

speaker: David Thomas
text: But the demand side is not being addressed.

speaker: David Thomas
text: If you look at um interest rates,

speaker: David Thomas
text: we've I mean the the UK house housing market has delivered up to 250,000 homes per annum a number of times since the 1980s.

speaker: David Thomas
text: This year, I think we're going to be sub 200,000 homes, around about that level.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So the reality is we've seen demand drop off quite dramatically over the last two or three years.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So trying to get back to that 250,000 level is very, very important.

speaker: David Thomas
text: And I say, you know, affordability is a key thing.

speaker: David Thomas
text: But but it's also about confidence.

speaker: David Thomas
text: You know, you you touched on the way that expectations have changed in terms of interest rates.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So this this lack of a a long runway to build confidence,

speaker: David Thomas
text: and really to get the market moving, that that is a big challenge.

speaker: David Thomas
text: And people who are first-time buyers can defer that decision.

speaker: David Thomas
text: People who are planning on moving up can defer that decision.

speaker: David Thomas
text: And people who are downsizing can defer that decision.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So the reality is we need that confidence and we need that momentum to get the market going.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: How might

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: you think the changes in the jobs market might affect that? Because not only just what we're going through economically and business decisions in reaction to higher prices, government policy, whatever that might be.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: But the way the world is changing as well.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: Is there a a risk for the housing market there if the growth of artificial intelligence means there's a whole different array of jobs that are out there?

speaker: David Thomas
text: Well, I think first of all, if you if you look at the industry over the last 20 years, you know, the industry will have been consistently saying that we have a lot of challenges around skills.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So the availability of skills in terms of young talent coming into the industry is a challenge.

speaker: David Thomas
text: If you look at the volumes that would have come into the industry in the 70s and 80s in terms of brick players, electricians, plumbers and so on.

speaker: David Thomas
text: People coming in on trade apprenticeships.

speaker: David Thomas
text: Then we've seen real challenges in terms of maintaining those volumes.

speaker: David Thomas
text: Um there's there's a much, much wider range of opportunities for young people, whether it be in terms of further education or different jobs.

speaker: David Thomas
text: Technology jobs, for example, which wouldn't have existed in the same way in the 70s or 80s.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So attracting people into the industry is a big challenge.

speaker: David Thomas
text: One that we feel the industry is really facing into and is trying to Is is it though?

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: I do wonder.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: Because you're you're about the third boss in the space of a week that we've spoken to who have talked about their industry facing challenges in recruitment.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: At some point, do you sort of not have to go the industry hasn't either sold itself well enough?

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: It's not providing people with the opportunities in the right places, you're you're not reaching the people who could be working for you.

speaker: David Thomas
text: Well, I think it's a combination.

speaker: David Thomas
text: I mean, one is, you know,

speaker: David Thomas
text: we are out there, we are recruiting, we are trying to expand numbers that we bring in as we seek to grow the business.

speaker: David Thomas
text: But also we've got to change method of production.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So more factory-based production.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So we we have and many of the other house builders have factories for production that 10 years ago we didn't have.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So producing timber frame, for example, in factories where we're we've got a center of production and that we're then shipping the frames around the country for production.

speaker: David Thomas
text: That can make significant changes to what we're doing on site, so bringing new technologies into our production line is allowing us to reduce labor content on site.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: So does that mean fewer staff overall, fewer workers overall?

speaker: David Thomas
text: It it will reduce our requirements in terms of onsite employees, switching from onsite to to offsite in in factories.

speaker: David Thomas
text: Because our business is expanding, so we've set out a very clear expansion plan.

speaker: David Thomas
text: Then we will in overall terms be employing more people.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: What what's the job onsite that we you would expect the role on site that you would expect to see fewer of in a few years time?

speaker: David Thomas
text: Well, the big area that we're challenged with on site is with regard to brick players, for example.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So that's been called out in a number of studies as being one of our biggest challenge areas.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: But is also something that can be replaced by factory production.

speaker: David Thomas
text: Correct.

speaker: David Thomas
text: And therefore we we can address that, you know, we have a lead time to allow us to address that.

speaker: David Thomas
text: And therefore looking at alternate solutions to uh bricks.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: But is is that okay?

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: I know for you know, brick layers listening to this, there may be concerns about what their skills may be required for.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: But is that just a natural innovation of industry?

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: That and an example of how something like AI and the advance of technology will replace some of those skills.

speaker: David Thomas
text: Yeah, I think I think that is a reasonable illustration of that.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So, you know, you you see introduction now to things.

speaker: David Thomas
text: One is brick laying robots, which in the UK, I would say I have limited application in residential space.

speaker: David Thomas
text: But for commercial buildings where you've got maybe a very long run of bricks to be laid, then robots have been used.

speaker: David Thomas
text: Firstly.

speaker: David Thomas
text: And then secondly using alternate cladding products that are, let's say, brick simulation.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So, so both of those are innovations.

speaker: David Thomas
text: I think if you're a brick layer looking at that, certainly anytime in the near term,

speaker: David Thomas
text: there's not going to be any shortage of demand for brick layers, that landscape isn't going to change dramatically in the short term.

speaker: David Thomas
text: But we need to invest in these factories so that in the medium to long term, What is medium term?

speaker: David Thomas
text: I I would I would say that, you know,

speaker: David Thomas
text: five to 10 years and then longer term beyond 10 years.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So I think you'll you'll see a revolution beyond 10 years in terms of the way that the industry, um, builds homes on site.

speaker: David Thomas
text: And the amount of offsite production that takes place.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: What would your advice be to a 25-year-old brick layer at the moment?

speaker: David Thomas
text: That it's it's going to be a very, very good career because when you look at the shortage of brick layers and you look at the requirements for brick layers in in all respects,

speaker: David Thomas
text: um then I think it would be a long time before you would be predicting that there is actually too many brick layers for the work that is required.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: Another aspect, because the housing market

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: reaches so many people's lives in in so many different ways.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: Another aspect of artificial intelligence doing people's work and those and then therefore people

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: either having to look for different type of jobs, or maybe those jobs not as many jobs being out there.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: So a question everybody's asking.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: Is is that a risk for the housing market for your ability to see the demand in people buying homes?

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: The fact that the jobs market of your customers is changing.

speaker: David Thomas
text: Yeah, I mean, I mean, to to the extent that uh people see as a result of that that, you know, average income levels or income levels reduce,

speaker: David Thomas
text: then clearly that that is a factor in terms of who can afford to buy homes.

speaker: David Thomas
text: But I see it as being more of a transition.

speaker: David Thomas
text: Not something that's going to happen immediately.

speaker: David Thomas
text: And I think when you look at AI, when you look at technology generally for our industry,

speaker: David Thomas
text: there are some really, really big opportunities for us in terms of how we can apply technology on our sites.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So to just to give one example, you know, we see that drone technology can do a lot for us in relation to looking at the way that our sites are developed to ensure that roads and all infrastructure are in in in exactly the right location.

speaker: David Thomas
text: To be able to measure that rapidly and accurately using drones.

speaker: David Thomas
text: And also site security can be challenging and therefore again drone technology can allow us to monitor a large volume of sites on a relatively low cost basis.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So there's lots and lots of areas of opportunity.

speaker: David Thomas
text: And I think that's what companies need to look at.

speaker: David Thomas
text: For us, I don't think it's so much about us displacing labor with um technology as such.

speaker: David Thomas
text: I think it's more about where we can use technology to to better advantage.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: Just summing all this up.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: There's a lot going on, isn't there?

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: In people's lives who are looking to buy a home, move home at the moment.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: You you've worked through a fair few crisis just in the last 17 years that you've had.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: Is that right number at at Barrett?

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: And um you are departing as chief executive very shortly.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: How does the environment now compare to some of the challenges of COVID, of Brexit, of political turbulence that we've seen in that period of time?

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: Even the back of the financial crisis, where you began at Barrett.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: How does now compare?

speaker: David Thomas
text: Well, I think first of all, I'll say I'm very, very proud of the business that we've built at Barrett Redro.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So we we have a large business.

speaker: David Thomas
text: We have three very, very strong customer brands, Barrett, David Wilson and Redro.

speaker: David Thomas
text: I think they all have a very clear definition within the marketplace.

speaker: David Thomas
text: And I think we're financially very strong.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So if you go back to when I joined in financial crisis, I mean, I came into the business as the group finance director.

speaker: David Thomas
text: I was um, you know, I think I was selected because I had history of distress and I'd seen distressed environments in different sectors,

speaker: David Thomas
text: and I'd undertaken a lot of refinancing.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So to put it in context, when I joined,

speaker: David Thomas
text: we had debts of about 1.6 billion and our most recent balance sheet position, you know, we had no debt in our balance sheet position.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So the reality is we've seen a dramatic transformation of the balance sheet position and we've seen a huge expansion of our customer reach over that period of time.

speaker: David Thomas
text: So I I feel that as a business,

speaker: David Thomas
text: uh as you said, I'm I'm sort of going to sign off later this year and Dean Banks is coming in as our our new chief executive.

speaker: David Thomas
text: And I feel that I'm leaving the business in a stronger position than the position I found it in.

speaker: David Thomas
text: But I also recognize that there are still, as we've discussed today, there are still lots of challenges for the industry, there's lots of challenges for Barrett Redro to face into.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: Just notable, you you mentioned before that it's in the 80s that you you got on the housing ladder.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: Yes, somebody born in the 1960s.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: I don't normally get into the the detail of people's backgrounds, but I just with housing, it's people's age and the opportunities they've had.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: Is resonates so much with people.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: Do you feel like your generation were lucky?

speaker: David Thomas
text: I I yeah, I go go back to the point I talked about, you know, our our generation were given huge advantages.

speaker: David Thomas
text: You know, the government made a decision that uh encouraging people onto the housing ladder had lots of good rationale behind it.

speaker: David Thomas
text: Whether it be, you know, economic, social, rationale.

speaker: David Thomas
text: And so, you know, I touched on the schema.

speaker: David Thomas
text: The scheme was a was a massive investment in the future.

speaker: David Thomas
text: And you know, I benefited from that, my my wife and I benefited from that in terms of getting onto the ladder.

speaker: David Thomas
text: And then clearly property prices in the UK, as I said, early from the 1980s have run way, way ahead of normal inflation.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: And your advice to somebody now of a similar age,

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: who might be, you know, at the time, maybe that you bought your first home,

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: now is thinking about that, what what what should they be doing?

speaker: David Thomas
text: Well, I think that from my point of view, you know, owning your own home, um, you know, living your life in in your own home, I think has lots and lots of advantages.

speaker: David Thomas
text: But I do recognize that affordability is a real challenge.

speaker: David Thomas
text: And therefore looking at how how people can best budget, best manage their finances to be able to get onto the housing ladder.

speaker: David Thomas
text: I think is a is a positive thing.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: David Thomas, chief executive of Barrett Redro.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: Thank you so much for your time.

speaker: David Thomas
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: If you want to listen to more episodes,

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: check out our back catalog.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: If you hit subscribe, you'll get a new one delivered each week.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: And you can contact the team that make the show, email us on bigboss@bbc.co.uk.

speaker: Sean Farrington
text: With any suggestions you've got for Big bosses we should be speaking to.
